story:
When I was a child, my mother longed for the country, so my father saved, sold his shop, and traded the city for fields and livestock. The nights were loud with crickets and owls, and the work was harder than any ledger or counter. He learned the land with calloused hands and tired eyes, and I learned to watch him and worry, then to help. I wondered if he regretted it, but when Mom smiled, he smiled too, and that was my answer. A family moved down the road, and with them came a girl with long black hair, honey-brown eyes, and a yellow hat that bobbed like a small sun. After chores we roamed meadows and trees; on city days we shared candy and laughter.

Years unfolded. When my parents passed, I took over the farm, and she—Alya—stood beside me until grief softened. When hers passed, I did the same. We fell into a rhythm: her bread, my soup, our shared table. On slow days we wandered market stalls, treated ourselves to pastries, and traded stories with old friends and new merchants. In winter we sat by the fire; in illness we took turns tending beds and fields. We teased and bickered until laughter cut through it. She called me her “ball and chain,” all in fun, and I wore the joke like a secret ring. She was more than a friend; she was a partner. I wanted to make it official—but what could I give her that we didn’t already share?

I remembered her stories of the sea, of salted air and a crisp breeze, a little house where she could fish and swim and forget the clock. So I saved every coin from selling tools and deliveries until I could buy a piece of cliff where water hammered rock and the horizon felt close. I tried to build a house on my own, and failed. I asked our friends, and they came without hesitation. It was work like the farm—mortar that cracked, fences that slumped, stones that wouldn’t lift without three pairs of hands. We made mistakes, started over, rested when backs or ankles gave out. Alya probed, eyes sharp as a hawk’s; I lied badly and improvised distractions. A year later, the walls held. An official signed off. I stood on the cliff, asked her father, somewhere beyond the spray, for his blessing. The wind wet my cheek. I took it as a yes.

I rented a carriage. She stared out the window, quiet, hiding a smile that lit up her cheeks. At the cliff she ran ahead, yellow hat flying, just like when we were kids. “Is this for me?” she asked. “I hoped it would be for us,” I said. She held me and cried, joy tangled with grief for the farm we loved. We decided not to leave it; the cliff house would be our rest days. We married beside that house with our friends as witnesses. We returned to chores and shared meals, and now and then we slept by the sea. She taught me to fish.

In five years we had four children. We raised them among fields and gulls, and when it was their turn to run the farm, Alya and I moved to the cliff. Retirement was quiet and full—friends until they dwindled, children and grandchildren who knew our story by heart. Every day we sat in our chairs and watched the sun rise and set. After she passed, I kept the ritual until my last day, believing that maybe, in heaven, a little home waits for us at the water’s edge. If not, I’ll build another. With my friends, I would do it all again.
